---
title: |
  <div style="text-align:center">
    <img src="logo_b.png" width="350" /><br>
    Milestone Week-8<br>
    <span style="font-size:0.5em;">Mark Kirkpatrick DVM, MS</span>
  </div>
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
format:
  html:
    page-layout: full
    theme: cerulean
    df_print: paged
    toc: true
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: false
    code-fold: true
execute:
  message: false
  warning: false
  echo: false
embed-resources: true
params:
  event:
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

if (!require("pacman")) install.packages("pacman")
pacman::p_load(
  broom,
  tidyverse,
  dtplyr,
  gt,
  arrow,
  rmarkdown,
  lubridate,
  quarto, 
  arrow,
  survival,
  survminer,
  ggsurvfit,
  flextable,
  gtsummary,
  marginaleffects,
  scales,
  zoo
  )

events_formatted <- read_parquet(
  '../data/intermediate_files/events_formatted.parquet') 

animals <- read_parquet(
  '../data/intermediate_files/animals.parquet')

  
# load GC's functions
source('../functions/fxn_load_os_fxns.R')
fxn_load_os_fxns()


# create variables for inline code
farm_name <- unique(events_formatted$location_event)

date_max_pull <- max(events_formatted$date_event, na.rm = TRUE)
date_min_pull <- date_max_pull - years(1)

# pull solution data
source('../functions/fxn_milestone8_solution.R')

```

```{=html}
<!-- The objective of this milestone is to build on the report and functions you used last milestone to analyze culling data in 2 different ways. You will create the culling variables and then visualize the data. You will then use both logistic regression and survival analysis to explore the data. 
A few notes:  1) The logic is to create culling variables can be complex depending on the questions I have tried to be explicit in how I created the data set you should be using. 2)Any data (including farm name) that is reported in the text should use an inline text code chunk -->
```

Run this chuck below. It will open the example report in your browser.
You should delete this chunk in your final report

```{r}
#| label: open-html-r
#| eval: false

# 1. Define the file path
file_path <- "images/milestone_8.htm"

# 2. Open the browser
browseURL(file_path)

# =========================================================================
# Survey commands
# =========================================================================

# summary(animals)
# glimpse(animals)
# summary(events_formatted)
# glimpse(events_formatted)
# unique(events_formatted$event_type)
# unique(events_formatted$event)

```

# Report for r farm_name

-   This report shows data from the past year from
    ** date to
    end date **.

-   For the culling analysis cows were censored on
    **end date** if they were not culled
    before this date.

## Objective

Your objective is to recreate the solution_data by doing the following
with the events_formatted and animals table.

1.  Filter the events_formatted table to only include LAME and TRIM
    events
2.  Then create new variables for lesions using the
    **fxn_code_lesions()** function that you used from Github last
    milestone
    -   This function is automatically loaded for you already.
    -   use (protocol_var = protocols_letters1, remark_var =
        remark_letters1) inside the function
3.  Address the abscess warning in the function by coding anything with
    ab in remark_letters1 or protocol_letter1 as noninf and remove them
    from other.
4.  Run the following 3 functions to process the lesion data further and
    create variables you will need to use later on.
    -   **fxn_collapse_lesions**(lesions = all_lesions)
    -   **fxn_trim_vars**(location_event, id_animal, date_var =
        date_event, trimonly_var = trimonly)
    -   **fxn_dz_status**(disease_cols = diseases, event_filter =
        trimonly == 0, gap = 1)
        -   ***NOTE**: All the other functions are specific for lameness
            this one is **NOT** and can be used for other diseases!*
5.  Then join this data table with the animals table to get the
    date_left, date_sold and date_died variables
6.  Then create new culling variables as described below
    -   **date_censor :** this is the last date the cow would have been
        eligible to be culled and should be the last date and event was
        entered in the data set
    -   **days_to_cull:** days from event(trimming) to culling
    -   **days_censor_cull:** days from event to culling or censor_date
        if not culled
    -   **culled_ever:** 1 if the cow was culled, 0 if not culled
    -   **culled_last_trim:** 1 if the cow was culled after her trim
        without being trimmed again.
    -   **culled_30**: 1 if the cow was culled \<=30 days after her trim
        0 if not
7.  Visualize this culling data
8.  Create some summary statistics of this data using both logistic
    regression and compare that the provided survival analysis functions

## Your Code Creation

-   The code block has some hints to help you create the solution_data
    data frame.

```{r}
#| label: your code
#| echo: TRUE

# =========================================================================
# Data cleaning and conversion.  Create solution_data
# =========================================================================
  # summary(events_formatted_lt)
  # glimpse(events_formatted_lt)
  # unique(events_formatted_lt$remark_letters1)
  # unique(events_formatted_lt$protocols_letters1)
  # write_csv(events_formatted_lt,"excel_excuses.csv")

library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)

# Filter events_formatted for only LAME and TRIM
events_formatted_lt <- events_formatted[events_formatted$event %in% c("LAME", "TRIM"), ]

# Apply the function to events_formatted_lt
events_formatted_lt <- fxn_code_lesions(
  events_formatted_lt,
  event_var = event,
  protocol_var = protocols_letters1,
  remark_var = remark_letters1
)

# Convert abscess designations to a "1" in the column noninf
events_formatted_lt$noninf <- ifelse(grepl("^Ab", events_formatted_lt$protocols_letters1), 1, 0)
# Convert abscess designations to a "0" in the column other
events_formatted_lt$other  <- ifelse(grepl("^Ab", events_formatted_lt$protocols_letters1), 0, 1)

# Apply the three classifying FUNCTIONS
events_formatted_lt <-
  events_formatted_lt |>
  fxn_collapse_lesions(lesions = all_lesions) |>
  fxn_trim_vars(
    location_event,
    id_animal,
    date_var = date_event,
    trimonly_var = trimonly
  ) |>
  fxn_dz_status(
    disease_cols = diseases,
    event_filter = trimonly == 0,
    gap = 1
  )

# Join the 'animals' to 'events_formatted_lt' and add listed variables
events_formatted_lt <- events_formatted_lt %>%
  left_join(
    animals %>% select(id_animal, date_left, date_sold, date_died),
    by = "id_animal"
  )

events_formatted_lt <- events_formatted_lt %>%
  arrange(id_animal_lact, date_event) %>%   # primary sort
  group_by(id_animal_lact) %>%
  mutate(
    # Sequence number within cow (by date)
    seq_date = row_number(),

    # Second sort sequence: within cow, order by event + date_event
    seq_event = row_number(order(order(event), date_event)),

    # Last event for this cow?
    is_last = seq_date == max(seq_date),

    # Was the cow removed at the last event?
    culled_last_trim = if_else(is_last & !is.na(date_left), 1, 0)
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(
    # Global censor date
    date_censor = max(date_event, na.rm = TRUE),

    # Days to cull
    days_to_cull = as.numeric(date_left - date_event),

    # Days to censor if no cull
    days_censor_cull = if_else(
      is.na(days_to_cull),
      as.numeric(date_censor - date_event),
      days_to_cull
    ),

    # Ever culled?
    culled_ever = if_else(is.na(days_to_cull), 0, 1),

    # 30‑day outcomes
    culled_30 = if_else(!is.na(days_to_cull) & days_to_cull <= 30, 1, 0),
    sold_30  = if_else(days_to_cull <= 30 & !is.na(date_sold), 1, 0),
    died_30  = if_else(days_to_cull <= 30 & !is.na(date_died), 1, 0)
  ) %>%
  select(-is_last)


# Add cohort and classification columns
events_formatted_lt <- events_formatted_lt %>%
  mutate(
    # DIM category bins
    dim_cat = case_when(
      dim_event >= 270 ~ ">270",
      dim_event >= 211 & dim_event <= 270 ~ "211-270",
      dim_event >= 151 & dim_event <= 210 ~ "151-210",
      dim_event >= 91  & dim_event <= 150 ~ "91-150",
      dim_event >= 30  & dim_event <= 90  ~ "30-90",
      dim_event >= 0   & dim_event <= 29  ~ "<30",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),

    # Floor dates to month
    floordate_fresh = floor_date(date_fresh, "month"),
    floordate_event = floor_date(date_event, "month")
  )



# =========================================================================

# put your code here to create the required data frame.
# name it solution_data

# you will need to use these functions and vectors with these arguments at some point in your code.



# all_lesions <- c("dd", "footrot", 'wld', "sole_ulcer","injury", 
#                              "cork", "other", "hemorrhage",
#                              "sole_fracture", "toe_ulcer", "thin",
#                              "inf", "noninf", "toe", "axial", "lesion")
# 
# diseases <- c("inf", "noninf", "lesion")

# solution_data <- 

# fxn_collapse_lesions(lesions = all_lesions) |> 
#   fxn_trim_vars(location_event, id_animal,
#                 date_var = date_event,
#                 trimonly_var = trimonly) |> 
#   fxn_dz_status(disease_cols = diseases,
#                 event_filter = trimonly == 0)


```

```{r}
#| label: create-solution-data


# waldo::compare(solution_data, solution)



```

## Visualize Data

### Violin plot

Then on the solution_data table.

-   Create a violin graph that shows the distribution of time to culling
    for cows with any lesion.
    -   restrict data to
        -   just the past 12 months
        -   data from the cow's last trimming
        -   lactating cows only
        -   positive days_to_censor_cull numbers only
-   To compare your restricted data to the solution data use
    -   *waldo::compare(log_data, restricted_data)*

```{r}
#| label: visualize-culling
#| echo: true

library(ggplot2)

# Recode, Filter and Relabel
# =========================================================================

# Recode the status_lesion variable
events_formatted_lt <- events_formatted_lt %>%
  mutate(status_lesion = case_when(
    status_lesion %in% c("Chronic", "New", "Repeat") ~ status_lesion,
    TRUE ~ "Ctrl"
  ))

# Filter the dataset
plot_data <- events_formatted_lt %>%
  # Remove heifers
  filter(lact_group_5 != "Heifer") %>%
  
  # # Remove trimonly = 1.  Evaluate only lame
  # filter(trimonly == 0) %>%
  
  # Keep only last 12 months of events
  filter(date_event >= (max(date_event, na.rm = TRUE) %m-% months(12))) %>%
  
  # Keep only positive days_to_cull
  filter(!is.na(days_to_cull), days_to_cull > 0) %>%
  
  # Keep only the last event per cow
  group_by(id_animal_lact) %>%
  filter(seq_date == max(seq_date)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  
  # Relabel lesion categories
  mutate(
    status_lesion_label = case_when(
      status_lesion == "Chronic" ~ "Chronic Lesion",
      status_lesion == "Ctrl"      ~ "No Lesion",
      status_lesion == "New"     ~ "New Lesion",
      status_lesion == "Repeat"  ~ "Repeat Lesion",
      TRUE ~ "Unknown"
    )
  )

# Create Violin Plot with scale = "count"
# =========================================================================

plot_data <- plot_data %>%
  group_by(status_lesion_label) %>%
  mutate(n_obs = n()) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(plot_data, aes(
  x = status_lesion_label,
  y = days_to_cull,
  fill = status_lesion_label
)) +
  geom_violin(trim = FALSE, alpha = 0.7, scale = "count") +
  stat_summary(
    fun.data = function(x) {
      data.frame(
        y = median(x),
        ymin = quantile(x, 0.25),
        ymax = quantile(x, 0.75)
      )
    },
    geom = "pointrange",
    color = "black",
    size = 0.6
  ) +
  geom_text(
    data = plot_data %>% distinct(status_lesion_label, n_obs),
    aes(
      x = status_lesion_label,
      y = max(plot_data$days_to_cull) * 1.05,
      # y = 0, (original left side placement)
      label = paste0("n = ", n_obs)
    ),
    vjust = -0.5,
    color = "black",
    size = 4,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Time to Culling by Lesion Status",
    x = "",
    y = "Days to Cull",
    caption = "Violin scale = count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )


# Create Violin Plot with undefined scale
# =========================================================================

plot_data <- plot_data %>%
  group_by(status_lesion_label) %>%
  mutate(n_obs = n()) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(plot_data, aes(
  x = status_lesion_label,
  y = days_to_cull,
  fill = status_lesion_label
)) +
  geom_violin(trim = FALSE, alpha = 0.7) +
  stat_summary(
    fun.data = function(x) {
      data.frame(
        y = median(x),
        ymin = quantile(x, 0.25),
        ymax = quantile(x, 0.75)
      )
    },
    geom = "pointrange",
    color = "black",
    size = 0.6
  ) +
  geom_text(
    data = plot_data %>% distinct(status_lesion_label, n_obs),
    aes(
      x = status_lesion_label,
      y = max(plot_data$days_to_cull) * 1.05,
      # y = 0, (original left side placement)
      label = paste0("n = ", n_obs)
    ),
    vjust = -0.5,
    color = "black",
    size = 4,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Time to Culling by Lesion Status",
    x = "",
    y = "Days to Cull",
    caption = "Violin scale = not defined"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )




```

### Heat Map

-   For non infectious lesions create a heat map similar to this using
    the culled_30 variable.
-   Restrict the data using the same criteria as above.

```{r}
#| label: fig-example_heat
#| echo: true


# =========================================================================
# 1. Filter and prepare dataset
# =========================================================================
plot_data <- events_formatted_lt %>%
  filter(lact_group_5 != "Heifer") %>%                 
  filter(date_event >= (max(date_event, na.rm = TRUE) %m-% months(12))) %>%
  mutate(
    status_lesion_label = case_when(
      status_lesion == "Chronic" ~ "Chronic Lesion",
      status_lesion == "Ctrl"    ~ "No Lesion",
      status_lesion == "New"     ~ "New Lesion",
      status_lesion == "Repeat"  ~ "Repeat Lesion",
      TRUE ~ "Unknown"
    )
  )

# =========================================================================
# 2. Summarize: % culled within 30 days + count
# =========================================================================
heat_data <- plot_data %>%
  group_by(status_lesion_label, lact_group_5) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    pct_culled_30 = mean(culled_30, na.rm = TRUE) * 100
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(
    # Remove heat mapping for Repeat Lesion
    pct_culled_30 = ifelse(status_lesion_label == "Repeat Lesion", NA, pct_culled_30)
  )

# =========================================================================
# 3. Build heat map
# =========================================================================
ggplot(
  heat_data,
  aes(
    x = status_lesion_label,
    y = lact_group_5,
    fill = pct_culled_30
  )
) +
  geom_tile(color = "black", alpha = 0.8) +

  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(
      ifelse(is.na(pct_culled_30), "NA", round(pct_culled_30, 1)), 
      "%\n(n=", n, ")"
    )),
    color = "black",
    size = 4
  ) +

  scale_fill_distiller(
    palette = "YlOrRd",
    direction = 1,
    name = "% Culled Within 30 Days",
    na.value = "white"   # ensures Repeat Lesion tiles are blank
  ) +

  labs(
    title = "% Culled - 30 Days by Lesion and Lactation Group",
    x = "Lesion Status",
    y = "Lactation Group"
  ) +

  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1)
  )

# =========================================================================
# 2. Summarize: % died within 30 days + count
# =========================================================================
heat_data <- plot_data %>%
  group_by(status_lesion_label, lact_group_5) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    pct_died_30 = mean(died_30, na.rm = TRUE) * 100
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(
    # Remove heat mapping for Repeat Lesion
    pct_died_30 = ifelse(status_lesion_label == "Repeat Lesion", NA, pct_died_30)
  )

# =========================================================================
# 3. Build heat map
# =========================================================================
ggplot(
  heat_data,
  aes(
    x = status_lesion_label,
    y = lact_group_5,
    fill = pct_died_30
  )
) +
  geom_tile(color = "black", alpha = 0.8) +

  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(
      ifelse(is.na(pct_died_30), "NA", round(pct_died_30, 1)), 
      "%\n(n=", n, ")"
    )),
    color = "black",
    size = 4
  ) +

  scale_fill_distiller(
    palette = "YlOrRd",
    direction = 1,
    name = "% Died Within 30 Days",
    na.value = "white"   # ensures Repeat Lesion tiles are blank
  ) +

  labs(
    title = "% Died - 30 Days by Lesion and Lactation Group",
    x = "Lesion Status",
    y = "Lactation Group",
    caption = "This is not the full archive risk"
  ) +

  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1)
  )



```

# Modelling

At times it is useful to create a model to determine if the differences
between culling risks between groups are important. Since we are
modelling culling one approach is to use logistic regression to do this.
Interpreting the outputs from these models can be harder to explain as
typically they output odds ratios which aren't commonly used by people
other then researchers/data geeks. For this reason we should turn the
model outputs back into risks.

In the section below create a logistic model that models the impact of
non_infectious lesion status on culling at 30 days. - Name the model
output log_model. \_ Ignore lactation for this model. There are ways to
account for lactation but we will keep it simple for now. - We have
provide the code to make looking at the model output and to turn it into
risks.

```{r}
#| label: fig-example_logistic
#| echo: true

# =========================================================================
# 1. Filter dataset + create status_lesion_label
# =========================================================================
model_data <- events_formatted_lt %>%
  filter(lact_group_5 != "Heifer") %>%                                 # remove heifers
  filter(date_event >= (max(date_event, na.rm = TRUE) %m-% months(12))) %>%  # last 12 months
  # filter(noninf == 1) %>%                                              # retain only non-infectious (optional)
  mutate(
    status_lesion_label = case_when(
      status_lesion == "Chronic" ~ "Chronic",
      status_lesion == "Ctrl"    ~ "No Lesion",
      status_lesion == "New"     ~ "New",
      status_lesion == "Repeat"  ~ "Repeat",
      TRUE ~ "Unknown"
    ),
    status_lesion_label = relevel(factor(status_lesion_label), ref = "No Lesion"),
    culled_30 = as.numeric(culled_30)
  )

# =========================================================================
# 2. Fit logistic regression model
# =========================================================================
log_model <- glm(
  culled_30 ~ status_lesion_label,
  data = model_data,
  family = binomial(link = "logit")
)

summary(log_model)


# View model summary
summary(log_model)

# log_model <- # your model code goes here

log_model_results <- tidy(log_model, exponentiate = TRUE,
                      conf.int = TRUE) |> 
  rename(oddsratio = estimate) |> 
  mutate(
    # Format p-value to 4 decimal places
    p.value = number(p.value, accuracy = 0.00000001),
    # Optional: Format OR and CI for readability
    estimate = number(oddsratio, accuracy = 0.01),
    conf.low = number(conf.low, accuracy = 0.01),
    conf.high = number(conf.high, accuracy = 0.01)
  )

log_model_results |> 
  gt()

# =========================================================================
# Odds ratios
# =========================================================================

OR <- exp(coef(log_model))

# 95% confidence intervals for odds ratios
CI <- exp(confint(log_model))

# Combine into a clean table
OR_table <- data.frame(
  Term = names(OR),
  Odds_Ratio = OR,
  CI_lower = CI[, 1],
  CI_upper = CI[, 2]
)

OR_table

# =========================================================================
# Predicted probabilities
# =========================================================================

# Create a new dataset with one row per lesion category
newdata <- data.frame(
  status_lesion_label = levels(model_data$status_lesion_label)
)

# Predicted probabilities with 95% CI
pred <- predict(log_model, newdata, type = "link", se.fit = TRUE)

newdata$prob <- plogis(pred$fit)
newdata$lower <- plogis(pred$fit - 1.96 * pred$se.fit)
newdata$upper <- plogis(pred$fit + 1.96 * pred$se.fit)

newdata

# =========================================================================
# Plot predicted probabilities
# =========================================================================

ggplot(newdata, aes(x = status_lesion_label, y = prob)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), width = 0.15) +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted Probability of Culling Within 30 Days",
    x = "Lesion Status",
    y = "Predicted Probability"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)


```

### Logistic Model Risks

```{r}
#| label: fig-example_logistic_risks
#| echo: true

log_model_risks <- avg_predictions(log_model, 
                               variables = c("noninf"))

log_model_risks <- log_model_risks |>  
  select(noninf,
    Risk = estimate,
         conf.low,
         conf.high) |> 
  mutate(
    Risk = number(Risk*100, accuracy = 0.1),
    conf.low = number(conf.low*100, accuracy = 0.1),
    conf.high = number(conf.high*100, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) |> 
  gt()

log_model_risks


```

You will notice the risks from the logistic model should be the same as
the overall row in the heat map, but now we have confidence intervals
around them to give us a sense of the uncertainty of the risk.

## Survival Analysis

One problem with using logistic regression and risks to deal with
culling is that we are likely underestimating the risk of culling since
some cows may be censored before they are culled. In our example
censoring would occur if it has not been 30 days since a cow was
trimmed. A better approach to deal with this is to use survival
analysis.

Below we have provided code to create a Kaplan Meier survival curve and
table to look at the impact of non infectious lesions on culling risk
over time. You will notice that the risk estimates are higher now that
we are accounting for censoring. A good quick read about survival
analysis can be found
[here](https://www.emilyzabor.com/survival-analysis-in-r.html#kaplan-meier-plots)

```{r}
#| label: fig-example_km
#| echo: true

# survival analysis
# km anaylsys

# km_fit <- survfit2(Surv(time = days_to_censor_cull,
#                 event = culled_last_trim) ~ noninf, 
# data = log_data)
# 
# surv_plot <- km_fit |> 
#   ggsurvfit(
#     type = "risk"
#   )+
#   add_confidence_interval()+
#   add_quantile()+
#   scale_ggsurvfit()+
#   scale_fill_viridis_d(name = "Non-Infectious Lesion Status")+
#   scale_color_viridis_d(name = "Non-Infectious Lesion Status")+
#   labs(x = "Days to Culling after Trim",
#        y = "Percent of Cows Culled")+
#   theme_minimal()+
#   theme(legend.position = "bottom")
# 
# surv_plot

# =========================================================================
# Kaplan-Meier Survival Analysis
# =========================================================================

library(survival)
library(ggsurvfit)

# =========================================================================
# 1. Filter dataset + create status_lesion_label
# =========================================================================
surv_data <- events_formatted_lt %>%
  filter(lact_group_5 != "Heifer") %>%                                 # remove heifers
  filter(date_event >= (max(date_event, na.rm = TRUE) %m-% months(12))) %>%  # last 12 months
  # filter(noninf == 1) %>%                                              # retain only non-infectious (optional)
  mutate(
    status_lesion_label = case_when(
      status_lesion == "Chronic" ~ "Chronic",
      status_lesion == "Ctrl"    ~ "No Lesion",
      status_lesion == "New"     ~ "New",
      status_lesion == "Repeat"  ~ "Repeat",
      TRUE ~ "Unknown"
    ),
    status_lesion_label = factor(status_lesion_label)
  )

# =========================================================================
# 2. Fit Kaplan–Meier model
# =========================================================================
km_fit <- survfit2(
  Surv(time = days_censor_cull, event = culled_ever) ~ status_lesion_label,
  data = surv_data
)

# =========================================================================
# 3. Plot survival curves
# =========================================================================
surv_plot <- km_fit |>
  ggsurvfit(type = "risk") +
  add_confidence_interval() +
  add_quantile() +
  scale_ggsurvfit() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(name = "Lesion Status") +
  scale_color_viridis_d(name = "Lesion Status") +
  labs(
    x = "Days to Culling",
    y = "Percent of Cows Culled",
    title = "Kaplan–Meier Survival: Culling by Lesion Status"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

surv_plot





```

```{r}
#| label: fig-example_km_table
#| echo: true

# times are adjustable

surv_table <- km_fit |>
  tbl_survfit(
    times = c(7, 30, 60, 180),
    label = " ",
    type = "risk",
    label_header = "{time} Days"
  ) %>%
  as_flex_table() %>% # makes formatting and pdf better
  add_header_row(
    top = TRUE,
    values = c(
      "Non-Infectious Lesion Status",
      "% Culled (Confidence Interval) at",
      "",
      "", ""
    )
  ) %>%
  bold(i = 1, bold = TRUE, part = "header") %>% # bolds headers
  bold(i = 2, bold = TRUE, part = "header") %>%
  merge_at(i = 1:2, j = 1, part = "header") %>% # merges 1st row
  merge_at(i = 1, j = 2:5, part = "header") # merges top columns

surv_table


```

# Extension

For this week extension continue to create standard things you would
want to report or incorporate modeling techniques to explore the data

Ideas

-   Try the code with other diseases like mastitis or culture
    information
-   Try to use the survival code to get estimates for time to repeat or
    recheck?
